critic_raw:
This deep Q-learning vs. heuristic delivery routing experiment contains a self-defeating flaw in its test set policy that undermines the entire evaluation. Section 6 explicitly states: 'if the model does not perform as expected on the test data, slight parameter adjustments are permissible to ensure alignment with operational goals. This iterative refinement ensures that the final model is adequately robust for deployment.' This is a direct admission of test set driven tuning — adjusting model parameters based on test performance destroys the statistical validity of the test evaluation and produces an optimistically biased estimate of generalization performance. No 'slight' qualifications make test set tuning acceptable in a scientific evaluation.

Second, the feature preprocessing specifies that all transformers are 'fit on the combined training and validation data (months 1-12).' While this is substantially better than fitting on the full dataset, it still leaks validation set distribution information into preprocessing, which affects feature scaling applied to validation data during hyperparameter tuning. A fully correct implementation would fit transformers on training data only.

Third, the RL agent uses a 'reward' that includes customer satisfaction penalties computed from production ratings. In a simulated RL training environment trained on historical logs, customer satisfaction ratings are observed outcomes — using them in the reward function during offline training creates a look-ahead bias if satisfaction ratings become available after delivery completion rather than at routing decision time.

Finally, the baseline is described as 'the incumbent heuristic routing algorithm currently in production' with no tunable parameters. Comparing a tuned, optimized RL agent against a fixed production heuristic is not a fair scientific comparison — any improvement could reflect the effect of optimization effort rather than architectural superiority.
all_issues_raised: Explicit test set contamination: parameter adjustments are explicitly permitted based on test set performance, directly invalidating the statistical validity of the test evaluation
Preprocessing leakage: transformers fitted on combined training and validation data, contaminating validation-set feature scaling used during hyperparameter tuning
Reward look-ahead bias: customer satisfaction ratings (used in RL reward) are post-delivery outcomes that would not be available at routing decision time in deployment
Unfair baseline: untuned production heuristic compared against extensively hyperparameter-searched RL agent — improvement may reflect optimization effort rather than architectural advantage
Customer satisfaction as a soft constraint (not a hard exclusion) means RL can trade off service quality for delivery time in ways that may not be detectable within the evaluation framework
Critical test set contamination: the design explicitly permits parameter adjustments after observing test set performance, directly invalidating the test set as an independent holdout

Preprocessing leakage: transformers fit on months 1–12 (including validation period) rather than training-only months 1–9, incorporating validation-period statistics into training-phase feature normalization

Reward function and evaluation constraint misalignment: the DQN's satisfaction penalty threshold (4.5) may not be calibrated to the binary constraint (satisfaction ≥ baseline mean) applied at test evaluation

Extremely limited training (50–100 episodes) for a complex routing problem raises questions about whether the DQN policy has converged, making the comparison between undertrained DQN and production heuristic unreliable

No confidence intervals or significance tests are specified for the primary delivery time comparison, despite bootstrap CI being mentioned as the reporting method — the statistical comparison methodology is underspecified
Test set policy violation: the design explicitly permits parameter adjustments based on test set performance, invalidating the test set as an unbiased generalization estimate
DQN action space bounded by the route generation heuristic — the RL agent cannot discover routes beyond what the heuristic generates, creating methodological circularity in the comparison
Rolling 6-hour mean arrival feature referenced as computed from 'full dataset statistics' suggests this feature may incorporate test-period data despite the temporal split
50-100 training episodes is likely insufficient for DQN convergence given the complexity of the routing task and the size of the training dataset — convergence verification is not described

Customer satisfaction penalty in the reward function uses a fixed threshold (rating < 4.5) without prior analysis of the satisfaction rating distribution — a poorly calibrated penalty term can dominate training and prevent delivery time optimization
